planting.note: Direct sow in fall in very dry, well-drained, lean, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; cold stratification through winter improves spring germination.

directions: Bractless Blazingstar is a native perennial of the Loasaceae family endemic to the dry, sandy prairies and rocky open ground of the Great Plains, where it produces leafy, branching stems topped with white, star-shaped flowers 2 to 3 inches across — each bloom packed with dozens of long, showy stamens that glow in the fading evening light. The flowers open at dusk and emit a sweet nocturnal fragrance that draws hawk moths and night-flying native bees, making this an exceptional plant for moonlight and pollinator gardens. The barbed, Velcro-like hairs covering the leaves and stems are an adaptation for seed dispersal via animal fur and clothing. Direct sow in fall in extremely lean, sandy or gravelly, fast-draining soil in full sun; established plants develop deep taproots and are among the most drought-tolerant native perennials of the Great Plains, requiring no supplemental water once settled.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Native Plant Database

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in rocky, very well-drained, lean, calcareous or sandy soil in full sun; not suited to heavy clay or moist soils.

directions: Ten-petal Blazingstar is a spectacular Great Plains and Rocky Mountain foothills native, bearing the largest flowers of any Mentzelia native to the United States — creamy-white, 10-petaled blooms up to 4 inches across that open at dusk and fill the evening air with a strong, sweet fragrance, drawing hawk moths and other night pollinators from considerable distances. It grows on dry, rocky, calcareous or sandy bluffs and open slopes from Nebraska to Texas and New Mexico, forming an upright, branching perennial clump clothed in rough, barbed leaves that cling to passersby. The combination of enormous blooms and intensely sweet evening scent makes it an extraordinary centerpiece for night and moonlight garden designs. Direct sow in fall in lean, rocky, fast-draining, calcareous or sandy soil in full sun; established plants are extremely drought-hardy and resent supplemental irrigation or organically rich soils.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Native Plant Database

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in lean, very well-drained, rocky or sandy, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun; tolerates caliche and desert soils well.

directions: Adonis Blazingstar is a slender, much-branched native perennial of Texas, New Mexico, Arizona, and adjacent Mexico, growing on dry, rocky, calcareous or sandy desert hills and open slopes in the Chihuahuan and Sonoran Desert regions. Unlike its white-flowered Great Plains relatives, it produces numerous bright yellow to orange-yellow, 5-petaled flowers 1 to 1.5 inches across on airy, much-forked stems, blooming freely from spring through fall and opening in the afternoon and evening hours rather than strictly at night. The lighter, daytime-accessible blooms attract a broader range of native bees and butterfly visitors than the nocturnal Mentzelia species, making it a highly productive pollinator plant across a long season. Direct sow in fall in extremely lean, rocky or sandy, well-drained, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun; an outstanding xeriscape perennial for Texas Hill Country and Chihuahuan Desert native gardens.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Native Plant Database

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in very well-drained, lean, calcareous or sandy soil in full sun; plants resent wet feet and thrive in rock gardens and dry prairie restorations.

directions: Fendler's Bladderpod (formerly Lesquerella fendleri) is a compact, cushion-forming native perennial in the mustard family, native to dry, calcareous, rocky, or sandy open ground across Texas, New Mexico, Oklahoma, and adjacent states. In early spring — often before almost any other wildflower has opened — it covers itself in bright yellow, 4-petaled mustard flowers held just above a silver-gray mat of densely hairy, spatulate basal leaves; the flowers are followed by round, inflated, bladder-like seed pods that persist ornamentally on the plant. These early blooms are a critical first nectar source for native mining bees, mason bees, and other native pollinators emerging in late winter and early spring when forage is scarce. Grow in very well-drained, lean, calcareous or sandy soil in full sun; Fendler's Bladderpod is an excellent and underused native for rock gardens, dry native prairie mixes, and xeriscape plantings across the southern Great Plains.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Native Plant Database

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in rocky, lean, well-drained, dry to slightly moist soil in full sun; self-sows freely in open ground and adapts to both serpentine and non-serpentine soils.

directions: Mountain Jewel Flower is one of California's most architecturally distinctive native wildflowers, producing erect stems clothed in clasping, ear-lobed leaves that are perfoliate at the base, topped with a raceme of inflated, urn-shaped calyces enclosing purple to cream-white petals that emerge only partially from the swollen sepals — a flower form utterly unlike typical mustard blooms that gives the plant an alien, jewel-like beauty. It is native to dry, rocky mountain slopes, chaparral openings, and serpentine barrens across much of California, and is one of the few wildflowers able to thrive on harsh, heavy-metal-rich serpentine soils. Specialist native bees in the genera Eucera and Anthophora are important pollinators, and some bee species have evolved to specialize exclusively on Streptanthus pollen. Direct sow in fall in rocky, lean, well-drained, dry to slightly moist soil in full sun; plants self-sow reliably in open, gritty ground and are a striking and unusual choice for California native and serpentine garden plantings.
